tasks.title: Unique Values in a Dynamic List
tasks.description:
Pointers let a program manage data whose size is not known until runtime, which is common when reading user input or processing a file. Dynamic memory allocation makes it possible to create exactly as much storage as needed instead of guessing a fixed maximum size.

Read an integer `n` (`1 <= n <= 1000`), followed by `n` integers. Store the values in dynamically allocated memory, count how many distinct integers appear, and print that count on its own line. Two integers are considered distinct if they have different values. All allocated memory must be released before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5 3 7 3 2 7 → distinct count: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3. Output: 3

tasks.title: Reverse a Line In Place
tasks.description:
A string can be stored in a buffer and manipulated directly through pointers, which is useful in text processing tools. Reversing characters in place is a classic example of swapping values through memory addresses.

Read up to 200 characters of text on one line, not including the newline. Reverse the line in dynamically allocated memory and print the reversed text followed by a newline. The input line may be empty, and spaces must be treated as normal characters. All allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: hello world → text: hello world → dlrow olleh. Output: dlrow olleh

tasks.title: Running Sum Until Sentinel
tasks.description:
When data arrives one value at a time, pointers and dynamic arrays can help store the values while a loop processes them as they are read. A running sum is a simple cumulative total that changes after each new number is accepted.

Read integers one by one until the value `-1` appears; the sentinel `-1` is not part of the data. There will be at least one number before `-1`, and at most 1000 numbers total. Store the numbers in dynamically allocated memory, compute their sum, and print the sum on its own line. If the first value read is `-1`, print `0`. Free all heap memory before exiting.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 4 2 9 -1 → sum: 0 → 4 → 6 → 15. Output: 15

tasks.title: Count Characters by Class
tasks.description:
Character classification is a common task in text analysis, and pointers to buffers make it easy to inspect each character one at a time. By scanning a line of text, a program can separate letters, digits, spaces, and everything else.

Read one line of text of up to 300 characters, not including the newline. Count how many characters are letters, how many are digits, how many are spaces, and how many are other characters, then print the four counts on one line in that order separated by spaces. The line may be empty. Use only dynamically allocated memory for any copied text, and free it before the program exits.

tasks.title: Expand a Number List with Realloc
tasks.description:
Dynamic arrays are useful when the final amount of data is not known in advance, because memory can be expanded as needed. This is a practical technique in programs that read a stream of values and keep only the meaningful ones.

Read integers until `0` is encountered; the `0` is not part of the data. There will be at most 1000 nonzero integers. Store the nonzero values in a dynamically growing array, then print them in reverse order on one line separated by spaces. If no nonzero integers are read, print a blank line. Free all allocated memory before exiting.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5 -2 8 0 → stored values: 0 → 5 → 5, -2 → 5, -2, 8 → reversed: 8 -2 5. Output: 8 -2 5

tasks.title: Average of Positive Numbers
tasks.description:
Pointers are often used to pass around arrays so that a program can examine each value and decide whether it should contribute to a result. Filtering data with conditions is a useful foundation for many later problems in statistics and data cleaning.

Read an integer `n` (`1 <= n <= 1000`), followed by `n` integers. Compute the average of the positive numbers only; ignore zeros and negative values. Print the result with exactly two digits after the decimal point, or print `0.00` if there are no positive numbers. Store the input values in dynamically allocated memory and free it before the program exits.

tasks.title: Trim and Copy a Word
tasks.description:
Strings are usually stored as character arrays, and pointers make it possible to walk through them one character at a time. This is useful when cleaning user input before it is stored or compared.

Read one line of text of up to 200 characters, including spaces. Copy only the first contiguous word made of non-space characters into dynamically allocated memory, then print that word followed by a newline. If the line begins with spaces or is empty, print a blank line. Free all allocated memory before exiting.

tasks.title: Compare Two Dynamic Arrays
tasks.description:
Pointer-based arrays allow a program to keep multiple sequences of values and compare them element by element. This is a common pattern in data validation, where two lists must be checked for agreement under simple rules.

Read an integer `n` (`1 <= n <= 1000`), then read two lists of `n` integers each. Determine whether the lists are identical. Print `YES` if every corresponding pair of elements matches, otherwise print `NO`. Store both lists in dynamically allocated memory and free everything before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 3 1 2 3 1 2 4 → match result: YES → NO. Output: NO

tasks.title: Remove Vowels from Text
tasks.description:
Character-by-character processing is a natural fit for pointers and dynamically allocated buffers. It is often used to filter text, for example when simplifying input before storage or search.

Read one line of text of up to 300 characters, not including the newline. Remove all vowels `A E I O U a e i o u` from the line and print the remaining characters in their original order, followed by a newline. If the line becomes empty, print a blank line. Use dynamically allocated memory for the result and free it before exiting.

test_cases.expected_output:
Input: C Programming
Expected Output: C Prgrmmng

test_results.expected:
Input: C Programming
Expected Output: C Prgrmmng

tasks.title: Find the Last Occurrence
tasks.description:
A pointer can be used to scan through a sequence while remembering the most recent place where a condition was satisfied. This is a useful pattern in searching text or arrays when the last matching element matters more than the first.

Read an integer `n` (`1 <= n <= 1000`), followed by `n` integers and then one target integer `x`. Find the last position where `x` appears in the list using 1-based indexing. Print that position, or print `-1` if `x` does not appear. Store the list in dynamically allocated memory and free it before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 6 4 1 4 2 4 3 4 → last position: -1 → 1 → 3 → 5. Output: 5
